shortDescription: Keratoplasty in South Korea (PK, DALK, DSAEK, DMEK) to restore sight clouded by corneal disease, scarring, or endothelial failure.

sections.overview: A corneal transplant, or keratoplasty, replaces diseased, scarred, or clouded corneal tissue with healthy donor cornea to restore clear vision. The cornea is the transparent dome at the front of the eye, and when conditions such as keratoconus, Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy, corneal scarring from infection or injury, or failed earlier grafts damage it, light can no longer focus properly and sight deteriorates. South Korean tertiary eye centers offer the full modern range of techniques rather than a single one-size approach. Penetrating keratoplasty (PK) replaces the entire thickness of the cornea and is used for deep scarring or full-thickness disease. Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) replaces the front layers while preserving the patient's own healthy inner endothelium, which lowers rejection risk and suits conditions like keratoconus. For diseases affecting only the innermost layer, endothelial keratoplasty techniques such as DSAEK and DMEK transplant just that thin layer, allowing far quicker visual recovery through a small incision. The right technique depends on which layers of the cornea are affected, and a Korean ophthalmology team determines this through detailed imaging and examination. For international patients from Africa, the appeal lies in access to selective lamellar and endothelial procedures performed by high-volume specialists, often with shorter scheduling than in home countries. Outcomes vary by diagnosis and graft type, and no transplant can be guaranteed, but matching the procedure to the disease is a major reason results have steadily improved.
sections.candidacy: Good candidates are people whose vision loss comes from a corneal problem that glasses, contact lenses, or medication can no longer correct. Common indications include advanced keratoconus, Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy and other endothelial failures, corneal scarring from trauma or past infection such as herpes or bacterial keratitis, hereditary corneal dystrophies, and previously failed grafts. The choice between PK, DALK, DSAEK, and DMEK depends on which corneal layers are diseased, so a thorough preoperative assessment with corneal imaging and a full eye examination is essential. Candidacy also depends on the health of the rest of the eye: significant glaucoma, severe dry eye, retinal disease, or uncontrolled ocular surface inflammation can affect whether and which transplant is advisable, and these are evaluated before surgery. General health matters too, since you must tolerate anesthesia and commit to the postoperative steroid drops and follow-up that protect the graft. International patients should be realistic that recovery and rejection monitoring continue for months, requiring either an extended stay or reliable ophthalmic care at home. Honest discussion of your full history with the Korean team is the only way to confirm you are a suitable candidate.
sections.procedure_steps: Care begins with consultation and diagnostic testing, including corneal topography, thickness mapping, and a detailed slit-lamp examination, to confirm the diagnosis and select the technique. Donor tissue is supplied through an eye bank, where corneas are screened and stored under controlled refrigeration before use. On the day of surgery, the procedure is usually done under local or general anesthesia depending on the case and patient. In penetrating keratoplasty the surgeon removes a circular full-thickness disc of the diseased cornea and sutures a matching donor button into place with very fine stitches. In deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty the diseased front layers are removed while the patient's healthy inner endothelium is preserved, and donor anterior tissue is positioned over it. In endothelial techniques such as DSAEK and DMEK, only the thin inner layer is replaced through a small incision, and an air or gas bubble is placed inside the eye to hold the graft against the cornea while it adheres. Surgery typically takes from under an hour to a couple of hours depending on complexity. Afterward the eye is shielded, and you receive specific instructions, including positioning face-up for a period after endothelial grafts so the bubble supports the new tissue.
sections.recovery_timeline: Recovery depends heavily on the technique. Endothelial keratoplasty generally restores useful vision fastest, with many people noticing clearer sight within one to two weeks and continued stabilization over the following one to two months as the graft settles. Penetrating keratoplasty recovers much more slowly because the full-thickness sutures stay in for an extended period and the resulting astigmatism takes time to settle; vision can continue to change for roughly twelve to eighteen months as stitches are gradually adjusted or removed. DALK falls between these, avoiding endothelial rejection but still requiring patience as the surface heals. Across all types, antibiotic and anti-inflammatory steroid eye drops are used for months to suppress rejection and inflammation, and the tapering schedule is individualized. International patients should plan an initial stay in Korea long enough for the first critical reviews, then arrange ongoing monitoring at home. You will be taught the warning signs of rejection, often summarized as redness, sensitivity to light, vision change, and pain, and told to seek care urgently if they appear. Protecting the eye from rubbing and trauma, attending every follow-up, and using drops exactly as prescribed are the strongest things you can do to protect the graft.
sections.cost_breakdown: Costs for corneal transplantation in South Korea vary widely with the technique chosen, the complexity of the case, the hospital tier, and whether one or both eyes are treated. Published medical-tourism figures for keratoplasty at major Korean centers commonly fall in an approximate range of 12,000 to 18,000 US dollars per eye, while simpler or partial procedures are sometimes quoted lower. These figures should be treated as estimates, not quotes, because the final price depends on the surgical approach (PK, DALK, DSAEK, or DMEK), donor tissue and eye-bank fees, anesthesia, hospital stay, and the diagnostic workup. Beyond the surgical fee, international patients should budget for the preoperative consultation and imaging, postoperative medications, multiple follow-up visits, accommodation for an extended stay, flights, interpretation services, and travel insurance. Because graft monitoring continues for months, you should also factor in the cost of ongoing care once you return home. Many Korean hospitals with international healthcare centers provide written cost estimates after reviewing your records, which is the only reliable way to plan a budget. Always request an itemized estimate covering surgery, tissue, medication, and follow-up before committing, and confirm what is and is not included.
sections.why_korea: South Korea has become a leading destination for medical travel, with foreign patient numbers rising sharply in recent years and Seoul concentrating much of that volume in its tertiary hospitals. The sector is supported by national policy through the Korea Health Industry Development Institute (KHIDI), a government body under the Ministry of Health and Welfare, and its Medical Korea program, which registers institutions licensed to attract foreign patients and administers the Korea Accreditation Program for Hospitals Serving Foreign Patients (KAHF). For corneal transplantation specifically, the draw is the concentration of high-volume ophthalmology specialists in Seoul's large tertiary eye centers who routinely perform the selective lamellar and endothelial techniques (DALK, DSAEK, DMEK) alongside traditional penetrating keratoplasty, supported by established eye-bank tissue systems and modern corneal imaging. International healthcare centers at major hospitals coordinate scheduling, interpretation, and documentation for overseas patients, which matters for African patients managing travel logistics from abroad. Choosing a KHIDI-registered, accredited institution gives a verifiable baseline of standards. That said, donor tissue availability and eligibility are assessed case by case, outcomes depend on diagnosis, and Korea is a destination for the surgery, not a substitute for the months of follow-up that protect the graft after you go home.

faqs.q: What is the difference between PK, DALK, DSAEK, and DMEK?
faqs.a: They differ by how much of the cornea is replaced. Penetrating keratoplasty (PK) replaces the full thickness. DALK replaces only the front layers and keeps your own inner endothelium. DSAEK and DMEK replace just the thin innermost layer through a small incision. The right one depends on which corneal layers are diseased, decided after imaging and examination by your Korean ophthalmology team.

faqs.q: How long will I need to stay in Korea?
faqs.a: It depends on the technique and your healing. You should plan an initial stay long enough to cover surgery and the first critical follow-up reviews, which for endothelial grafts can be a few weeks and for penetrating keratoplasty may be longer. Because graft monitoring continues for months, most patients arrange ongoing eye care at home after returning. Confirm the expected timeline with your hospital.

faqs.q: Where does the donor cornea come from?
faqs.a: Donor corneas are supplied through eye banks, where the tissue is screened and stored under controlled refrigeration before transplantation. Availability and suitability are assessed case by case, so timing can depend on tissue supply. Ask your hospital's international center how donor tissue is sourced and how scheduling around tissue availability works for your specific procedure.

faqs.q: Can a corneal transplant be rejected?
faqs.a: Yes. Any corneal graft can be rejected, though risk varies by technique, with full-thickness PK generally carrying more endothelial rejection risk than lamellar approaches like DALK. You will use anti-inflammatory steroid drops for months to lower this risk and be taught the warning signs, commonly redness, light sensitivity, vision change, and pain. If any appear, seek eye care urgently. No transplant outcome can be guaranteed.

faqs.q: How soon will my vision improve after surgery?
faqs.a: It varies by technique. Endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK/DMEK) often gives clearer vision within one to two weeks and continued improvement over one to two months. Penetrating keratoplasty recovers far more slowly, with vision changing for roughly twelve to eighteen months as sutures and astigmatism settle. Your surgeon will explain the expected curve for your specific procedure and diagnosis.
